structured_choices.data_source: EHR database containing 100,000 patient records over 5 years. Patient eligibility: diagnosed with diabetes or prediabetes, age ≥18, baseline HbA1c recorded, no missing demographic or genomic data. Records are time-stamped; baseline assessment is the index date; 12-month progression is measured from index date onward.
structured_choices.label_definition: 12-month diabetes progression is defined as the change in HbA1c level (continuous) from baseline to 12 months post-index date (HbA1c_12m − HbA1c_baseline). This is a continuous regression target. Patients with <11 months of follow-up or missing 12-month HbA1c are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split by baseline HbA1c quartile and treatment status (medicated vs. unmedicated at baseline). This ensures both models see comparable distributions of disease severity and treatment exposure. Split proportions: 60% train (60,000), 20% validation (20,000), 20% test (20,000). Random stratified split is appropriate because: (1) the outcome distribution is heterogeneous across severity; (2) no temporal ordering is intrinsic to the hypothesis; (3) stratification prevents either model from training on a skewed subset of severity levels; (4) no data leakage risk with random split, as all records are independent observations from a cross-sectional cohort.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, genomic data standardization) are fit on the training set only. Fitted transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. No information from validation or test sets is used during preprocessing. This prevents data leakage from label statistics or covariate distributions outside the training set.
structured_choices.features: Gradient-boosted model: demographic features (age, gender, one-hot encoded ethnicity) + genomic features (standardized SNP values; diabetes-associated SNPs per literature review; missingness indicator for any missing SNPs). Linear regression baseline: demographic features only (age, gender, one-hot encoded ethnicity). Feature engineering: age is used raw; categorical ethnicity is one-hot encoded; genomic SNP data is z-score standardized (mean 0, SD 1 within training set) to ensure scale consistency. Missing SNPs are imputed using the training set mean (fit on train, applied to val/test); missingness is also flagged as a binary feature for the boosted model. No lagged or future treatment variables are included to avoid encoding the outcome.
structured_choices.model: Gradient-boosted decision tree regressor (LightGBM or XGBoost, hyperparameters tuned via validation set). Learning rate, tree depth, subsample ratio, and regularization (L1/L2) are tuned. Baseline: linear regression with L2 regularization (ridge), regularization strength tuned via validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Linear regression using demographic features only (age, gender, ethnicity). This baseline is appropriate because: (1) it directly tests whether adding genomic data improves over demographics alone, isolating the contribution of genomic features; (2) it represents current clinical practice in many settings; (3) it is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (regularization strength selected on validation set); (4) both models are evaluated on identical test data using the same metrics. The baseline is not a 'null' but a fair, well-tuned competitor.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the held-out test set. RMSE is the primary metric because: (1) it directly reflects prediction error in HbA1c units, which is clinically interpretable and operationally meaningful (e.g., an RMSE of 0.5% HbA1c vs. 1% has clear intervention implications); (2) it penalizes large errors, which is appropriate for patient safety (large prediction errors lead to inadequate intervention); (3) it is symmetric and does not favor over- or under-prediction, suitable for clinical triage; (4) stakeholders explicitly care about 'lower prediction error' for timely intervention. Hypothesis is supported if: RMSE_boosted < RMSE_linear with a pre-specified margin (e.g., ≥5% relative reduction, or ≥0.1 absolute HbA1c units), and the difference is statistically significant (95% CI of the difference excludes zero).
secondary_metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — robust to outliers; clinically interpretable in HbA1c units.
R² on test set — explains variance accounted for; allows comparison of model fit quality.
Stratified metrics by baseline HbA1c quartile — ensures the boosted model does not overfit to one severity group at the expense of others.
Prediction interval coverage and calibration plots — assesses whether confidence intervals are well-calibrated across the outcome range, important for clinical decision-making.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both the boosted model and baseline are tuned on the validation set using 5-fold cross-validation within the 20,000-record validation set. The model with the lowest mean CV RMSE on the validation set is selected. Validation set is held separate from training and used only for hyperparameter tuning, not for final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (20,000 records) is never used during training or hyperparameter tuning. After both models are finalized, they are applied to the test set once, and metrics are computed. No threshold tuning, post-hoc calibration, or feature re-engineering is performed after test set evaluation. A single test run ensures an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: treatment adherence. Mitigation strategy: (1) collect a proxy for adherence (e.g., medication refill frequency, treatment intensification during follow-up) and include it as a feature in both models. This ensures both models account for adherence equally, making it a covariate rather than a confounder. (2) Perform sensitivity analysis: stratify test set results by adherence level (high, medium, low) and verify that the boosted model's advantage over linear regression holds across adherence strata. If the advantage disappears in low-adherence patients, adherence may be a residual confounder. (3) Temporal effects of interventions: include a treatment history variable (e.g., date of treatment intensification, number of medication changes) as a feature for both models. This accounts for in-window interventions. Do not interpret this feature causally; it is a confounder to be balanced. No causal claims are made; the design tests association, not causation. Expected claim scope: 'The boosted model with demographic and genomic data achieves lower prediction error on this cohort than demographics alone,' not 'genomic data causes better predictions' or 'this model is ready for deployment.'

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Genomic + Demographic vs. Demographic-Only Diabetes Progression Prediction

### 1. Data Preparation
We source 100,000 patient EHRs from the healthcare database spanning 5 years. We apply the following inclusion criteria: (a) documented diabetes or prediabetes diagnosis; (b) age ≥18 years; (c) baseline HbA1c recorded within the EHR; (d) at least one HbA1c measurement 11–13 months after baseline (index date); (e) complete demographic data (age, gender, ethnicity); (f) genomic SNP data available or imputable. We exclude records with missing 12-month HbA1c or follow-up <11 months. This yields an analysis cohort of approximately 85,000–95,000 patients after QA checks.

The 12-month progression label is defined as the continuous change in HbA1c from baseline to 12 months (HbA1c_12m − HbA1c_baseline). No categorical discretization is applied; we model the continuous outcome.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split
We use a stratified random split to divide the cohort into three groups:
- **Training set:** 60% (≈51,000 records)
- **Validation set:** 20% (≈17,000 records)
- **Test set:** 20% (≈17,000 records)

Stratification is performed by (i) baseline HbA1c quartile and (ii) treatment status at baseline (medicated vs. unmedicated). This ensures both models train and evaluate on comparable distributions of disease severity and treatment exposure, preventing either model from overspecializing to a skewed population subset.

We use random (not temporal) stratification because the hypothesis compares model types, not temporal generalization. Baseline index dates are dispersed across the 5-year window, and no causal temporal ordering is intrinsic to the cross-sectional design.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
**Demographic features** (both models): age (continuous), gender (binary, one-hot encoded to prevent multicollinearity), ethnicity (categorical, one-hot encoded into k−1 dummies).

**Genomic features** (boosted model only): diabetes-associated SNPs identified from published GWAS studies (e.g., TCF7L2, PPARG, HHEX loci; approximately 20–50 SNPs based on clinical relevance). SNP data is standardized using z-score normalization (mean 0, SD 1) within the training set. Missing SNP values are imputed to the training set mean (imputation statistics fit on train, applied to val/test). A binary missingness indicator for each SNP is added to the feature set to capture any diagnostic or sample quality patterns.

**Potential confounders and mediators** (both models): treatment adherence proxy (refill frequency, medication intensification events), treatment history (date of any medication changes, number of regimen modifications during follow-up window). These are included as features in both models to balance their effect.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. No label statistics or out-of-sample covariate information is used during preprocessing, preventing leakage.

### 4. Models
**Proposed model:** Gradient-boosted decision tree regressor (LightGBM or XGBoost) trained on demographic + genomic features + confounders. Hyperparameters (learning rate, max tree depth, subsample fraction, L1/L2 regularization) are tuned via 5-fold cross-validation within the validation set.

**Baseline model:** Linear regression (ridge-regularized) trained on demographic features + confounders only. Regularization strength (alpha) is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation within the validation set, matching the tuning effort of the boosted model. This baseline is appropriate because it represents current clinical practice in many settings and isolates the contribution of genomic data.

Both models receive equivalent computational and hyperparameter tuning resources.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection
Within the 20,000-record validation set, we perform 5-fold cross-validation for each candidate hyperparameter configuration. The configuration yielding the lowest mean cross-validated RMSE is selected for final training on the full validation set. This ensures hyperparameters are chosen without peeking at the test set.

### 6. Evaluation
**Primary metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the held-out test set. RMSE reflects prediction error in HbA1c units and is clinically interpretable (e.g., 0.5% vs. 1.0% HbA1c difference). We report RMSE for each model and compute the relative improvement [(RMSE_linear − RMSE_boosted) / RMSE_linear]. We consider the hypothesis supported if RMSE_boosted < RMSE_linear by ≥5% relative improvement with a 95% confidence interval of the difference that excludes zero.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — robust alternative to RMSE.
- R² on test set — proportion of variance explained.
- Stratified RMSE by baseline HbA1c quartile — ensures no single severity group drives improvement.
- Calibration plots and prediction interval coverage — validates clinical utility.

### 7. Confound and Sensitivity Controls
Treatment adherence and in-window interventions are included as features in both models, balancing their effect. We stratify test-set results by adherence level to confirm the boosted model's advantage is not an artifact of differential adherence between groups. We also report results stratified by ethnicity and gender to identify any disparities in model performance.

### 8. Scope and Limitations
This design tests **association**, not causation. We do not claim that genomic data *causes* better predictions or that the model is deployment-ready. We evaluate prediction accuracy in this specific cohort; external validation on independent sites and populations would be required for generalization claims.
